critique.summary: The article reports that Ubuntu 26.04 LTS will introduce visible asterisks for each character typed at the sudo password prompt, ending a 46-year tradition of silent input. This change is enabled by default in the new Rust-based sudo implementation (sudo-rs) and aims to improve user experience despite some security concerns.
mainPoints: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS will show asterisks for each sudo password character typed, replacing the traditional silent input.
The original silent prompt was a security measure to prevent shoulder surfing by hiding password length.
The change is enabled by default in sudo-rs, a Rust rewrite of sudo, which Ubuntu adopted starting with version 25.10.
The security risk of showing password length is considered minimal by developers, who argue that shoulder surfing is unlikely to be mitigated by hiding asterisks.
Users can revert to the classic silent prompt by modifying the sudoers configuration.
This change is part of broader modernization efforts in Ubuntu 26.04, including GNOME 50 on Wayland, Linux kernel 7.0, and more Rust-based utilities.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are accurate and verifiable. The sudo utility was indeed created in 1980 with a silent password prompt, and Ubuntu has recently transitioned to sudo-rs, a Rust implementation. The decision to enable pwfeedback (showing asterisks) by default in sudo-rs is documented in upstream commits and bug reports. The security arguments presented reflect common discussions in the Linux community about usability versus security trade-offs. The ability to revert the behavior via sudoers configuration is standard practice. The timeline and technical details align with publicly available information about Ubuntu's development plans and sudo-rs changes.
considerations: The article does not mention potential impacts on accessibility, such as how the change affects users relying on screen readers or other assistive technologies.
It does not explore in detail the perspectives of security experts who might argue for maintaining silent input in high-security environments.
The discussion does not address whether similar changes are planned or implemented in other major Linux distributions beyond Linux Mint and Ubuntu.
The article does not provide direct quotes or perspectives from Canonical representatives or the sudo-rs development team beyond commit messages and bug report summaries.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Ubuntu 26.04's change to show asterisks when typing sudo passwords, ending decades of silent password entry. Commenters debate the UX improvements versus security concerns, automation impacts, and alternatives for feedback without revealing password length.

comments.author: dtech
comments.text:
This is such a good decision. It's one of those things that's incredibly confusing initially, but you get so used to it over the years, I even forgot it was a quirk.

In the modern world there is no plausible scenario where this would compromise a password that wouldn't otherwise also be compromised with equivalent effort.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately captures the UX improvement and the minimal security tradeoff, reflecting a balanced and informed opinion.

comments.author: caijia
comments.text:
One angle nobody's mentioned: this breaks pexpect-based automation scripts. A lot of Python deployment tooling (Fabric, Ansible's become_pass, custom provisioning scripts) uses pexpect to detect the sudo prompt by matching the ":" pattern and checking that no output follows. Asterisk feedback changes the output pattern.

Not a reason to keep silent passwords — it's the right UX change. But worth knowing if you maintain automation that interacts with sudo prompts over SSH.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced technical perspective on automation impacts, an important consideration often overlooked in UX changes.

comments.author: dietr1ch
comments.text:
I like the idea of showing keystrokes, but I think that a 1:1 entry has arguably better alternatives.

The default entry on xsecurelock[^0] shows a character jumping on a line between keystrokes, which works well on giving key press feedback while visibly obfuscating password length,

    ________|_______________________    // after pressing a key it'd move around,
    ___________________|____________
Also, for anyone looking into preserving this last resort obfuscation behaviour you can do it with,

    # /etc/sudoers
    Defaults !pwfeedback
On NixOS (using sudo-rs),

    security.sudo-rs.extraConfig = ''
      # NixOS extraConfig
      # ===========
      Defaults !pwfeedback
    '';
I've got to say, if you were able to see me typing, you can probably record me doing so, bug my USB keyboard, or buy a $10 wrench. I guess for people streaming it might be worth it? I don't think it's a big enough deal to warrant the fuss around this change though, it's just an ok UX improvement that could be slightly better at retaining the sense of security.

[^0]: https://github.com/google/xsecurelock#options
comments.analysis: Offers constructive suggestions for balancing feedback and security, showing awareness of alternative UX designs and practical security concerns.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
They could have just made it an option to enable the new behavior. There was no need to change the default.

As for security: 'shoulder surfing' may not be as much of a concern, but watching a livestream or presentation of someone who uses sudo will now expose the password length over the internet (and it's recorded for posterity, so all the hackers can find it later!). They've just introduced a new vulnerability to the remote world.
comments.analysis: Raises valid security concerns about password length exposure in remote or recorded contexts, highlighting a tradeoff in the change.

comments.author: jiehong
comments.text: This fixes another issue with that if you make a typo in your password, you don't know how many characters you need to delete, but now you would.
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies a common usability problem with silent password entry that the change helps to resolve.

comments.author: pvillano
comments.text:
How much information is there in knowing the length of someone's password?

If we know the password's length, it saves us from guessing any shorter passwords. For example, for a numeric password, knowing the length is 4 saves us from having to guess [blank], 0-9, 00-99 and 000-999. This lowers the number of possibilities from 1111 to 1000. The password has 90% of it's original strength. A [0-9a-zA-Z] password retains 98% of it's original strength
comments.analysis: Provides a clear, quantitative explanation of the security impact of revealing password length, showing that the strength loss is relatively minor.

comments.author: timhh
comments.text:
I did this!

I didn't actually know that Mint had enabled this by default. That would have been a useful counterpoint to the naysayers.

If you want the original behaviour you don't actually need to change the configuration - they added a patch afterwards so you can press tab and it will hide the password just for that time.

> The catalyst for Ubuntu’s change is sudo-rs

Actually it was me getting sufficiently pissed off at the 2 second delay for invalid passwords in sudo (actually PAM's fault). There's no reason for it (if you think there is look up unix_chkpwd). I tried to fix it but the PAM people have this strange idea that people like the delay. So I gave up on that and thought I may as well try fixing this other UX facepalm too. I doubt it would have happened with the original sudo (and they said as much) so it did require sudo-rs to exist.

I think this is one of the benefits of rewriting coreutils and so on in Rust - people are way more open to fixing long-standing issues. You don't get the whole "why are you overturning 46 years of tradition??" nonsense.

If anyone wants to rewrite PAM in Rust... :-D

https://github.com/linux-pam/linux-pam/issues/778
comments.analysis: Shares insider knowledge about implementation details and configuration options, adding valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: caditinpiscinam
comments.text: It surprises me how many applications don't give you the option to see your password in plain text as you enter it. The messaging around password security is that we should be making them complex and unique, but then password UIs make that as difficult to do as possible. Is visual password stealing really a bigger issue than weak passwords / password reuse?
comments.analysis: Raises a broader UX issue about password entry design and security messaging, encouraging reflection beyond the sudo context.

comments.author: vandyswa
comments.text:
When I wrote the login program for my VSTa microkernel, I took a page from the CDC side of the world--it echoes a _random_ (but small, non-zero) number of *'s. So you get feedback, but indeed peering over your shoulder will not disclose password length.

And yes, it remember how many it echoes so backspace works correctly.
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting alternative approach to password feedback that balances usability and security, showing practical experience.

comments.author: leni536
comments.text:
sudo is not the only thing that prompts for password in the terminal. There is at least passwd and ssh.

I value ctrl+U a lot more for password prompts than the visual feedback, it's even used by GUI on Linux.
comments.analysis: Highlights the broader context of password prompts in terminals and suggests alternative usability features that might be more valuable.
